title: King Lear
author: Shakespeare
note: "O you mighty gods! / This world I do renounce, and in your sights / Shake patiently my great affliction off." Gloucester's prayer before his "fall" might be the most honest moment I've read in a while. Here's a man who's lost everything: his eyes, his son, and his place in the world, and he's not screaming or cursing, he's just asking if it's okay to stop. What gets me is that Shakespeare never treats that impulse as shameful. Four hundred years before we had language for mental health, he wrote a scene that basically says: I see your pain, and I'm not going to pretend it isn't real.

title: OpenClaw
note: I've been playing around with OpenClaw recently. I have it manage my schedule and studying, so it updates me (from my phone) whenever I wake up, etc. Bought a server so it can run 24/7, but my honest review is that its not too worth the money. API tokens cost too much money and the skill wall to optimize is quite high. I've cut down significantly, but it still costs me ~$20 a month.

title: Bitch: a history
author: Karen Stollznow
note: The bit about guys at old universities calling whoever made the tea "the bitch", and even complimenting him for doing it well shows the word was never really about one thing. It (and language, generally) has always been about who's doing what, and whether the people around them think that's a cool thing to be doing or not.

title: I spoke to AI agent Claude
author: Bernie Sanders
note:
Unsettling to watching a politician steer the AI from a position in under eight minutes. The tool being discussed as a threat is simultaneously demonstrating its own persuadability. If a Senator can do this on camera, what could less visible actors do to public opinion (over millions of conversations)?

AI has been proven to be biased, and as we trust chatbots more and more, I wonder how these biases will play into politics.

title: Building Evo 2: A Frontier DNA Language Model
The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas
author: Ursula K. Le Guin
note:
Le Guin's "The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas" presents a child who has been kicked, abandoned, and imprisoned by humans. While recklessly neglected and intentionally brutalized by the people of Omelas, the author includes that "[The child] is afraid of the mops. [The child] finds them horrible." It is afraid of two mere inanimate mops in the corner of the room.

The child is afraid of the mops because they are useless. Mops are cleaning tools: designed to remove dirt and restore, yet they stand uselessly in the corner of the same room where the child sits in "damp…cellar dirt". They are not frightening because they hurt the child, but because they could clean but do not. The mops are, in this reading, a figure for the people of Omelas themselves, who: "would like to do something for the child," but never do. They, like the mops, neglect their duty.

What makes the child's situation so unbearable is not only the suffering itself, but that its suffering could be immediately remediated, yet nothing happens. Le Guin presents the sin of omission and neglect. Just as the child is fearful of these self-defeating mops, readers should be fearful of the self-defeating people of Omelas. The people of Omelas have a moral duty to protect the innocent, as they possess the knowledge and power to prevent the child's agony. Despite not doing any direct harmful action, by choosing to live their lives while the child suffers, they become as useless as a mop that will not clean, and just as frightening.

title: Bullet in the Brain
author: Tobias Wolff
note: A man's entire life distilled into the fraction of a second. One of the great American short stories.

title: Do Not Go Gentle into That Good Night
author: Dylan Thomas

title: The Art of Stillness
author: Pico Iyer · TED
note: A beautiful meditation on how stillness is where we find clarity. Fascinating.
